## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (World)
On August 7, 1998, the incredibly massive, heavily coordinated, and deeply horrific Al-Qaeda terrorist bombings simultaneously aggressively struck the United States embassies in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, and Nairobi, Kenya. The massive, devastating truck bombs violently killed 224 people and aggressively injured over 4,000, predominantly innocent Africans. This incredibly massive atrocity officially brought Osama bin Laden to absolute global infamy, triggering massive US cruise missile retaliations and heavily setting the deeply tragic stage for the September 11 attacks.

On August 7, 1942, the incredibly massive, historically definitive, and fiercely bloody Guadalcanal Campaign officially and aggressively commenced during World War II in the Pacific. The United States Marines executed a massive, deeply heroic amphibious landing on the Japanese-held island of Guadalcanal. The incredibly brutal, six-month massive land, sea, and air battle fundamentally exhausted the absolute, massive offensive capability of the Imperial Japanese Navy, decisively shifting the absolute strategic initiative to the massive Allied forces.

## SECTION 9 — This Day in History (India)
On August 7, 1905, an incredibly massive, absolutely historic, and profoundly revolutionary public meeting was aggressively held at the Calcutta Town Hall. In fierce, unyielding retaliation against Lord Curzon's deeply offensive, massive impending Partition of Bengal, furious nationalist leaders aggressively and formally launched the massive *Swadeshi* movement. This monumental decision aggressively mandated the absolute boycott of British goods and fiercely galvanized the massive Indian populace into an absolute, subcontinent-wide mass economic and political revolution against colonial tyranny.

On August 7, 1941, Rabindranath Tagore, the absolute, towering, and universally revered genius of Indian literature and culture, tragically passed away in Calcutta. An incredibly prolific massive poet, philosopher, playwright, and composer, he had heavily revolutionized Bengali literature and achieved massive historical immortality as the very first non-European Nobel Laureate in Literature. His incredibly profound, massive legacy and deeply humanist philosophy left an absolute, eternal imprint on the massive, cultural architecture of modern India.
